title: Adjective Endings (Nominativ & Akkusativ)
summary: How adjective endings change depending on case, gender, and what kind of article (if any) comes before them.

sections.heading: Three patterns, one goal
sections.body:
When an adjective comes directly before a noun (die gute Idee), it needs an ending. Which ending depends on three things: the case (here: Nominativ or Akkusativ), the gender/number of the noun, and what precedes the adjective:
1. A der-word (der, die, das, dieser, jeder, welcher...) → weak declension.
2. An ein-word (ein, kein, mein, dein...) → mixed declension.
3. No article at all → strong declension.
The underlying logic: German only needs ONE strong signal of case+gender per noun phrase. If the article already gives that signal clearly, the adjective ending can be weak (-e or -en). If there's no article, or the article is ambiguous, the adjective ending has to carry that signal itself.

sections.heading: Weak declension — after der/die/das
sections.body: After a definite article (or dieser/jeder/welcher), adjectives end in -e or -en almost everywhere, because the article already shows case and gender.

sections.heading: Mixed declension — after ein/kein/mein...
sections.body: Ein-words don't mark gender clearly in the masculine nominative or the neuter nominative/accusative (ein could be any of those) — so there the adjective ending has to step in and show it (-er, -es). Everywhere else, the ending is weak (-e or -en), just like after der-words.

sections.heading: Strong declension — no article
sections.body: With no article at all (often with uncountable nouns, plurals, or in headlines), the adjective ending itself has to carry the full case/gender signal — it mirrors the endings that der/die/das/die would have.

exercises.prompt: Der klein___ Hund schläft. (Nominativ, Maskulin, nach "der")
exercises.explanation: Weak declension after "der": masculine nominative ending is -e.

exercises.prompt: Ich sehe den klein___ Hund. (Akkusativ, Maskulin, nach "den")
exercises.explanation: Weak declension: masculine accusative ending after "den" is -en.

exercises.prompt: Ein klein___ Hund schläft. (Nominativ, Maskulin, nach "ein")
exercises.explanation: Mixed declension: "ein" doesn't show gender in the masculine nominative, so the adjective takes -er to signal it.

exercises.prompt: Ich habe einen klein___ Hund. (Akkusativ, Maskulin, nach "einen")
exercises.explanation: "einen" already clearly marks masculine accusative, so the adjective is weak: -en.

exercises.prompt: Das ist ein neu___ Auto. (Nominativ, Neutrum, nach "ein")
exercises.explanation: Mixed declension: "ein" doesn't show gender for neuter either, so the adjective takes -es.

exercises.prompt: Wir kaufen frisch___ Brot. (Akkusativ, Neutrum, kein Artikel)
exercises.explanation: Strong declension (no article): the adjective must carry the neuter signal itself, -es, matching what "das" would show.

exercises.prompt: Meine gut___ Freundin kommt heute. (Nominativ, Feminin, nach "meine")
exercises.explanation: Feminine nominative/accusative after an ein-word is always weak: -e.

exercises.prompt: Kalt___ Wasser schmeckt im Sommer gut. (Nominativ, Neutrum, kein Artikel)
exercises.explanation: Strong declension: no article, so the adjective takes -es to show neuter nominative.

exercises.prompt: Sie hat keine neu___ Ideen. (Akkusativ, Plural, nach "keine")
exercises.explanation: Plural after an ein-word (keine) is weak: -en, just like after "die".

exercises.prompt: Die klein___ Katze schläft. Which ending?
exercises.explanation: Weak declension after "die": feminine nominative ending is -e.

exercises.prompt: Er trinkt schwarz___ Kaffee. (kein Artikel, Akkusativ, Maskulin)
exercises.explanation: Strong declension, masculine accusative: the ending must show it, -en (matching "den").

exercises.prompt: Ich lese ein spannend___ Buch. Which ending?
exercises.explanation: Mixed declension, neuter accusative after "ein": -es (ein doesn't mark neuter, so the adjective does).

exercises.prompt: Which article type requires the STRONGEST adjective endings overall?
exercises.explanation: With no article, the adjective alone must signal case and gender, so it uses the strongest set of endings.

exercises.prompt: Warum braucht "ein" im Maskulinum Nominativ ein -er am Adjektiv?
options: Weil "ein" Maskulinum, Neutrum und teils andere Formen nicht eindeutig markiert
Weil alle Adjektive nach "ein" immer -er bekommen
exercises.explanation: "ein" looks the same for masculine and neuter, so it can't show gender on its own — the adjective ending has to.

exercises.prompt: Der neu___ Kollege heißt Tom. (Nominativ, Maskulin, nach "der")
exercises.explanation: Weak declension after "der": masculine nominative is always -e.

exercises.prompt: Ich mag den neu___ Kollegen. (Akkusativ, Maskulin, nach "den")
exercises.explanation: Weak declension: masculine accusative after "den" is -en.

exercises.prompt: Guter Kaffee macht wach. (Nominativ, Maskulin, kein Artikel) — which article-less pattern is this?
exercises.explanation: No article at all → strong declension, where the adjective mirrors the definite article's ending (-er, like "der").

exercises.prompt: Wir haben ein klein___ Problem. (Nominativ, Neutrum, nach "ein")
exercises.explanation: Mixed declension, neuter nominative after "ein": -es.

exercises.prompt: Der neue Kollege sieht den neue Chef.
exercises.answer: Der neue Kollege sieht den neuen Chef.
exercises.explanation: "den Chef" is masculine accusative, so the adjective needs the weak accusative ending -en, not -e.

exercises.prompt: Ich trinke gerne kalte Wasser.
exercises.explanation: "Wasser" is neuter with no article (strong declension), so the adjective needs -es, not -e.

exercises.prompt: Ein guter Freundin hat mir geholfen.
exercises.answer: Eine gute Freundin hat mir geholfen.
exercises.explanation: "Freundin" is feminine, so it needs "eine" (not "ein") and the weak feminine ending -e (not -er).

exercises.prompt: Wir besuchen unseren alte Großvater.
exercises.explanation: "unseren Großvater" is masculine accusative after an ein-word, which needs the weak ending -en, not -e.

exercises.prompt: Put the words in the correct order.
exercises.explanation: Subject (Die Familie) + verb (hat) + accusative object with mixed-declension adjective (eine kleine Wohnung, feminine accusative = -e).

exercises.prompt: Put the words in the correct order.
exercises.explanation: Subject with weak-declension adjective (Der gute Mann, masculine nominative = -e) + verb + object.

exercises.prompt: Put the words in the correct order.
exercises.explanation: Strong declension (no article): "frisches Brot" — neuter accusative ending -es, since there's no article to carry the signal.
